# Hood Uber Proves the Need. We Built the Fix.
## Pudding Article #23 — Bishop B049
## Slug: `pudding/hood-uber-proves-the-need`

---

Twenty-one thousand people in San Antonio organized their own rideshare network through Facebook groups. No app. No company. No safety net.

They call it Hood Uber.

A March 2026 news report documented what this looks like in practice: cheaper fares than Uber and Lyft, cash and Venmo payments, drivers working dawn to dark six days a week. # Pudding #26: Making Affordability a Status Symbol
## Innovation: #2127 — Cooperative Status Economics
## Bishop B053

---

<!-- Deploy to: cephas_content_registry, category: pudding, style: pudding -->

**FoundersCard costs $495 a year. Liana Banyan costs $5.**

One buys you hotel upgrades. The other builds you an economy.

---

## The Old Status Symbol

FoundersCard markets itself to 250,000 "entrepreneurs and business professionals." For $495 per year, you get discounts at Hilton, United, Virgin Atlantic, Waldorf Astoria, and Wynn. Forbes called it "a community for leaders." The Wall Street Journal said it "soothes stress with elite access."

What does it build? Nothing. What does it produce? Nothing. What do the 250,000 members own? A membership card and a slightly cheaper hotel room.

### More Info (~300 words)

Traditional manufacturing works backwards. A company designs a product, manufactures thousands of units, and then hopes customers buy them. When they don't, the result is markdowns, waste, and write-offs. The entire system optimizes for prediction — and predictions are usually wrong.

Design Democracy reverses the sequence. A member or team submits a product design to the platform. The community reviews it, discusses it, and votes. Designs that earn enough support enter the production pipeline. Designs that don't are archived — not dele

# Pudding #35: Design Democracy — The Vote Before the Build
## Why Members Decide What Gets Manufactured

---

### At a Glance (~50 words)
Most platforms decide what to build in a boardroom. Liana Banyan puts product designs to a member vote before a single unit is manufactured. Design Democracy means no overproduction, no guessing, and no warehouse full of things nobody wanted. The community votes. The factory listens.

---

### More Info (~300 words)

# Pudding #49: The Switzerland Rule

*How a cooperative handles the two topics everyone says you should never discuss.*

---

### The Rideshare Extraction Model

Rideshare companies like Uber and Lyft have a structural problem: the driver provides the most expensive piece of equipment — the car — and the company captures the margin from its use. The driver absorbs:

- Depreciation (the car loses value with every mile)
- Fuel
- Insurance
- Maintenance
- Wear and tear on a personal asset used for commercial purposes

The company provides: an app and a payment system. The company earns: 25-40% of every fare.

Every social media platform optimizes for engagement. The difference is what engagement means.

On TikTok, engagement means the algorithm learned that you watch cooking videos at 11 PM and now serves you food content to keep you scrolling through midnight. # Pudding #82 — The Sixty-Five Dollar Moat

*Why micro-entity provisional patents are the most underrated weapon in the individual creator's arsenal.*

---

The conventional wisdom on patents is clear: they are expensive, slow, and only useful if you can afford to enforce them. A non-provisional utility patent costs $1,600 in government fees at the standard rate, plus $10,000-$30,000 in attorney fees. The process takes 2-3 years. And if someone infringes, you need litigation funding that starts at six figures.

All of this is true for the standard path. But the standard path is not the only path.

# Pudding #84 — The Coalition

*When small businesses stop competing and start combining their purchasing power.*

---

A single coffee shop buys 50 pounds of beans a month. The distributor charges full price. A coalition of twelve coffee shops buys 600 pounds a month. The distributor offers a volume discount. The savings go back to the shops, not to a middleman.

This is not a new idea. Group purchasing organizations — GPOs — have operated in healthcare, hospitality, and food service for decades. What is new is making the mechanism available to any collection of small businesses on a cooperative platform for $5 per year per member.

A rideshare driver on a conventional platform picks up a passenger for a $25 fare. The platform takes 25-40%. The driver receives $15-$19. The passenger has no idea how much the driver actually earned, and the driver has no idea how the platform calculated its cut.

Modern professional networks abandoned this model. LinkedIn charges $360-$720 per year for premium features. Industry associations charge $200-$2,000 in annual dues. # Pudding #92 — The Beacon

*Location-based discovery that turns a walk through your neighborhood into an economic act.*

---

Geocaching proved that people will walk miles to find a hidden container in the woods. Pokemon Go proved that people will walk miles to catch a digital creature on their phone. Both demonstrated the same principle: attach a reward to a location, and people will go there.

Liana Banyan's Beacon system applies this principle to local economic discovery. A Beacon is a location-based point tied to a real business, a real product, or a real service. Walk within range, and your phone registers the discovery. The Beacon is not a coupon. It is not an advertisement. It is a signal that something exists at this location that a member of the cooperative placed there.

# Pudding #93 — The HexIsle

*A game world where the economics are real and the hexagons are the point.*

---

Most games with economies are designed to extract money from players. Loot boxes, premium currencies, pay-to-win mechanics, battle passes — the game's economy exists to convert player engagement into platform revenue. The player's time is the raw material. The platform's profit is the product.

HexIsle inverts this. It is a game world built on hexagonal geography where the economic mechanics mirror the cooperative's real economics. The currencies in the game — Credits, Marks, Joules — are the same currencies used across the rest of the platform. Effort in the game generates value that is recognized outside the game.

# Pudding #94 — The IP Ledger

*Every idea documented. Every creator credited. Every innovation tracked from napkin sketch to patent filing.*

---

When an employee at a large company has an idea, the company owns it. The employment contract includes an intellectual property assignment clause — anything you invent on company time, using company resources, or related to company business belongs to the company. The employee might get a plaque. The company gets the patent.

Liana Banyan's IP Ledger exists because the cooperative is not an employer. Members are not employees. In November 2025, the Liana Banyan platform was a monolithic React application. Pages lived in a flat directory. Components were organized by convenience, not by function. By April 2, 2026 — five months later — the platform had been reorganized into 23 distinct domains, each with its own pages, components, hooks, libraries, routes, and type definitions. Commerce. wading_diving_in_layer:
# Pudding #96 — The Librarian

*An AI knowledge management system that remembers everything so the agents don't have to.*

---

AI agents have a memory problem. Each session starts fresh. The context window — however large — is finite. When a codebase has 580 tables, 139 edge functions, 470 pages, and 2,130 documented innovations, no single session can hold all of it. The agent that built the housing domain yesterday does not remember doing it today.

The Librarian solves this. It is a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server — a TypeScript application that indexes the entire platform and exposes 23 specialized tools for querying the knowledge base. Instead of reading every file at session start, an agent calls `brief_me` with a task description and receives a compact, task-scoped context packa

# Pudding #97 — The Cost Plus Twenty

*The three-word pricing rule that makes extraction structurally impossible.*

---

Every platform has a take rate. Uber takes 25-40%. Etsy takes 6.5% plus listing fees plus advertising fees. Amazon takes 8-45% depending on category. DoorDash takes 15-30% from restaurants. In each case, the take rate is set by the platform, adjusted by the platform, and optimized for the platform's revenue — not the seller's earnings.

Liana Banyan has a take rate too. It is Cost+20%. Three words. One rule. Applied universally to every transaction on the platform, from a $5 subscription to a $5,000 manufacturing order.

# Pudding #98 — The One-Way Valve

*Why Credits never cash out to dollars — and why that is the most important design decision in the entire system.*

---

On every other platform with an internal currency, there is a cash-out button. Twitch bits can be converted to dollars. Roblox Robux can be converted to dollars. Even airline miles — the original platform currency — can be redeemed for cash-equivalent purchases. The conversion creates a speculative layer: people accumulate the currency not because they want to use it, but because they want to convert it later at a favorable rate.

Airbnb started as a simple idea: people have spare rooms, travelers need affordable places to stay, connect them. The platform's cut was modest at first. Then it grew. Service fees climbed to 14-20% of the booking total. Cleaning fees became a second price hidden until checkout. Dynamic pricing algorithms raised rates during peak demand. The hosts kept less. A Blizzard is not a Conk. A Conk is a discrete failure — a 403, a missing module, a build step that returns non-zero. A Conk has a location. You know where it happened and roughly why.

## Bishop B082 | April 5, 2026

## THE PROOF

Every platform you've ever used makes you do two things at once: figure out what it IS and figure out how to USE it.

That's because most platforms have one shell. One frame. One interface that somehow has to serve the person who just arrived AND the person who's been there for three years. The result is a compromise nobody asked for: the new user sees too many buttons, and the veteran can't find anything because the buttons keep moving to make room for the "simplified" onboarding.

We solved this by building two shells.

## The Pudding

Imagine you mow a lawn. The grass is cut. The homeowner is satisfied. The work is done.

Now imagine someone tells you that because of market conditions, the lawn you mowed last Tuesday is only half-mowed now. That the effort you put in has been retroactively reduced. That the sweat on your shirt counts for less today than it did when you earned it.

You would call that absurd. Work that has been completed does not un-complete itself. A meal you cooked was still cooked. A shed you built is still standing.

title: Pudding #196: REF Staff — Read, Don't Re-write
canonical_topic: Blood, Sweat & Tears — platform origin narrative series
definition: *The architectural safety property that makes the cooperative's substrate trustworthy by construction.* --- ## At a Glance
founder_voice_quotes: This is Your World. Shape it, or Someone Else WILL.
We made it impossible for the policy to be violated by construction.

skipping_stones_layers.at_a_glance:
When you give an AI access to your material, the standard worry is: *what if it changes something?*

Not "what if it summarizes badly" or "what if it hallucinates" — those are about output quality. Th
skipping_stones_layers.more_details: *The architectural safety property that makes the cooperative's substrate trustworthy by construction.* --- ## At a Glance
skipping_stones_layers.in_depth:
if the AI modifies my files?* What if it overwrites the original? What if I can't tell what it changed? What if my source-of-truth gets corrupted?

This worry is justified. Many AI systems *can* write to source. The user has to trust the policy.

In our cooperative, the answer is different. We don't ask you to trust the policy. **We made it impossible for the policy to be violated by construction.**

We call our agents **REF Staff**. Reference. Or Referee. Never Re-writer.

# Pudding #196: REF Staff — Read, Don't Re-write

*The architectural safety property that makes the cooperative's substrate trustworthy by construction.*

---

## At a Glance

When you give an AI access to your material, the standard worry is: *what if it changes something?*

Not "what if it summarizes badly" or "what if it hallucinates" — those are about output quality. The deeper worry is: *what if the AI modifies my files?* What if it overwrites the original? What if I can't tell what it changed? What if my source-of-truth gets corrupted?

This worry is justified. Many AI systems *can* write to source. The user has to trust the policy.

In our cooperative, the answer is different. We don't ask you to trust the policy. **We made it impossible for the policy to be violated by construction

y. *Then* commit.

The canary is the metaphor: a small, disposable proxy you send into the dangerous territory first, so that when something goes wrong, you find out before the full operation breaks. The canary's death warns the miners. The canary saves lives.

In modern software, the canary is a tenth of your traffic, a small subset of your servers, a five-percent rollout. The principle holds: send the small version first; catch problems before they're large.
